title: Variables and Data Types
summary: Store information in variables and work with numbers, text, and true/false values

sections.title: Variables are like labeled boxes
sections.content: Imagine you have a set of storage boxes in your garage. Each box has a label (the variable name) and holds something inside (the value). In Python, you create a box by writing a name, an equals sign, and the thing you want to store. The box can hold different kinds of items: numbers, words, or true/false flags. Unlike other languages, Python figures out what kind of thing you're storing without you having to tell it.

sections.title: Creating and using variables
sections.content: This example stores a student's name, age, and enrollment status in variables
sections.code:
# A string variable holds text — notice the quotes around the value
student_name = "Maria Gonzalez"  # Store the student's full name
# An integer variable holds a whole number — no quotes needed
student_age = 21  # Store age as a number you can do math with
# A boolean variable holds True or False — like a light switch
is_enrolled = True  # Store whether the student is currently enrolled
# Print combines text and variables using a comma
print("Name:", student_name)  # Display the label and the value
print("Age:", student_age)  # Show the age on screen
print("Enrolled:", is_enrolled)  # Show enrollment status
# Expected output:
# Name: Maria Gonzalez
# Age: 21
# Enrolled: True

sections.title: Basic data types explained
sections.content: Python comes with four essential data types you'll use constantly. Strings (str) are sequences of characters wrapped in quotes — like a name or an address. Integers (int) are whole numbers — positive, negative, or zero — that you can add, subtract, or multiply. Floats (float) are numbers with decimal points, useful for prices or measurements. Booleans (bool) are the simplest: just True or False, typically used as flags or answers to yes/no questions.

sections.title: Don't mix types by accident
sections.content: A common mistake is trying to combine a string and a number with +. Python won't guess what you mean. Wrong: print("Age: " + 25) raises a TypeError. Correct: convert the number with str() first: print("Age: " + str(25)). Or use a comma: print("Age:", 25).

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: You're building a simple profile card for a library system. Create variables for a book's title (string), number of pages (integer), price (float), and whether it's checked out (boolean). Then print each piece of information labeled on its own line.
hints: Which data type would you use for a price that includes cents?
What happens if you try to print a number without converting it to string first?
